title: normalizeEncoderOptions silently drops convenience fields when their value is 0
description: In normalizeEncoderOptions the condition `if (v !== undefined)` is false for `v === 0`, so callers who pass `progressiveDc: 0`, `groupOrder: 0`, `modular: 0`, or `brotliEffort: 0` have their setting silently dropped and never forwarded to advancedFrameSettings. Zero is a valid and meaningful value for every one of those ids (e.g. modular:0 forces VarDCT, progressiveDc:0 disables DC layers). The fix is `if (v !== undefined && v !== null)` — but the real guard should be `v != null` (covers both null and undefined).
evidence: The check `v !== undefined` correctly handles the value 0: `0 !== undefined` evaluates to `true`, so 0 IS forwarded to advancedFrameSettings. The map type is `Array<[number | undefined, number]>` so null cannot appear here. No real failure scenario exists for 0-valued convenience fields.

title: normalizeEncoderOptions is called twice when using the top-level createEncoder shorthand
description: createEncoder (line 359) calls createNativeCodecFacade(loadNativeBinding()). loadNativeBinding calls adaptBindingCreators, which wraps createEncoder to call normalizeEncoderOptions (line 429). createNativeCodecFacade then wraps that already-adapted binding and calls normalizeEncoderOptions again (line 348). On the second call the idempotency guard `!adv.some(x => x.id === id)` prevents duplicate entries, so correctness is preserved for the convenience fields. However if a caller passes advancedFrameSettings with an id that matches a convenience field, the second normalization silently ignores the convenience field instead of erroring, masking a misconfiguration. Any future convenience field added to the map is also silently no-op'd on double-call for that id.
evidence: Verified in src/index.ts: `createNativeCodecFacade` at line 347-350 calls `normalizeEncoderOptions(options)` then `binding.createEncoder!(normalized)`, but `binding` here is the adapted binding from `adaptBindingCreators` which itself calls `normalizeEncoderOptions` at line 428-431. The top-level `createEncoder` export at line 359 exercises this double-normalization path. The idempotency guard prevents corruption but masks misconfiguration where caller advancedFrameSettings conflict with convenience fields.

title: Native DecoderClose reads decodeExtraChannels with default=true but JS layer defaults it to false
description: In DecoderClose (native.cc:1658) `decodeExtraChannels` is read with fallback `true`: `GetBoolProp(env, options, "decodeExtraChannels", true)`. The JS layer in both adaptBindingCreators (index.ts:421) and createNativeCodecFacade (index.ts:342) merges `decodeExtraChannels: false` as default before passing options to the native addon. If someone bypasses the JS facade and calls the native addon directly (e.g. in tests via a custom binding stub), or if the merge is ever dropped, extra channel decode is inadvertently enabled. The native default should match the JS default (false).
evidence: Verified at native.cc:1658 the default is `true` and at index.ts:342 and 421 the JS layer explicitly merges `decodeExtraChannels: false`. Any direct caller of the native binding (bypassing the JS facade) will unexpectedly decode extra channels, wasting memory and time.

title: decompression-bomb test assertion is missing await — the rejection is never observed
description: On line 496, `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow("image exceeds maxPixels")` is missing `await`. In Bun (and Jest), `expect(...).rejects` returns a Promise; without `await` the test function returns before the promise settles, so the assertion is never checked and the test passes unconditionally whether or not the error is thrown. The correct form is `await expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)`. This makes the decompression-bomb guard untested.
code_excerpt:
await decoder.push(encoded);
    expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow("image exceeds maxPixels"); // ← missing await
evidence: Confirmed at codec.test.ts:496: the `await` is absent before `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)`. The decompression-bomb guard (`maxPixels: 1`) is therefore untested — a regression that removes the maxPixels enforcement would pass this test silently.

title: JXL_DEC_FRAME_PROGRESSION early-return skips further frames in multi-frame progressive decode
description: When `!emit_every_pass && target != ProgressionTarget::Final` (line 1201-1203), DecodeAll returns `true` after the first FRAME_PROGRESSION event. For animations with multiple frames this discards all subsequent frame data — no final events are ever emitted for the remaining frames. The progression event fires per-frame, so returning early after the first progression event of a multi-frame animation produces a single partial progress event and no final event for any frame including the last. A caller relying on the `final` event to consume animation frames would receive nothing.
evidence: Verified: `JXL_DEC_FRAME_PROGRESSION` fires per frame in animation. The early return at line 1201-1203 exits DecodeAll entirely, so for a multi-frame animation with `progressionTarget: 'dc'` and `emitEveryPass: false`, only the first frame's progression event is captured and `JXL_DEC_FULL_IMAGE` events for all frames are never reached. Any caller relying on progress-then-final for animated JXL with non-Final targets will receive no final frames.

title: Progress event region.w/h echoes transformed ev_info dimensions instead of the original request region
description: In the FRAME_PROGRESSION handler (line 1184-1193), the region object echoed on the progress event uses `ev_info.width` and `ev_info.height` for `w` and `h`. By this point `ev_info` is a copy of `info` (line 1149), which still holds the full image dimensions (transform_fused_into has not yet been called on ev_info, only on the snapshot). So the echoed region.w/h reflect the original full image width/height, not the requested crop dimensions.
evidence: `transform_fused_into` takes `ev_info` by reference (`ImageInfo& info`) and mutates `ev_info.width = eff.rw; ev_info.height = eff.rh` (native.cc:627-628) before the region echo at lines 1190-1191. The region echo therefore uses the post-transform crop dimensions, not the full image dimensions. When the flush fails (`flushed=false`), no event is emitted at all (guarded by `if (prog_ev_ab != nullptr)` at line 1182).

title: rgba16 2x downsample accumulates four uint16 values into uint16 without widening, risking overflow
description: In the rgba16 ds==2 fast-path (lines 748-751), four uint16_t values are summed: `(p00[0] + p01[0] + p10[0] + p11[0] + 2) >> 2`. In C++ `uint16_t + uint16_t` promotes to `int` (32-bit), so four 65535 values sum to 262142 — well within int range and the result fits in uint16.
code_excerpt:
dest_row[x * 4 + 0] = (p00[0] + p01[0] + p10[0] + p11[0] + 2) >> 2;
// p00[0] is uint16_t; sum promoted to int by C++ rules; max = 4*65535+2 = 262142 < INT_MAX — safe
evidence: The finder itself correctly notes this is safe due to C++ integer promotion rules: 4 × 65535 + 2 = 262142, well within int32 range. No overflow is possible. The finding acknowledges safety in the description. Not a real bug.

title: guardDecoderOptions does not validate that maxPixels is a positive integer
description: guardDecoderOptions validates region geometry but does not check that maxPixels (when provided) is a positive finite number. A caller passing `maxPixels: -1`, `maxPixels: 0`, `maxPixels: Infinity`, or `maxPixels: NaN` would either disable the guard or cause undefined behavior at the `px > max_pixels` comparison in native.cc (max_pixels is cast from double to uint64; NaN casts to 0, allowing any image through; negative doubles cast to large uint64 via two's-complement, also disabling the guard).
evidence: Verified: native.cc:1662 casts `maxPixels` via `static_cast<uint64_t>(GetNullableNumberProp(...))`. NaN casts to 0 in C++ static_cast, disabling the pixel-count guard entirely. Negative values cast to a very large uint64, also defeating the guard. The JS `guardDecoderOptions` does not validate `maxPixels` at all.

category: path traversal / arbitrary native module load
title: User-supplied prebuiltPath/sourcePath passed directly to require() without sanitization, enabling arbitrary native addon load
description: loadNativeBinding() accepts NativeLoaderOptions where prebuiltPath and sourcePath are caller-controlled strings that are passed verbatim to Node's require(). A caller (or a consumer reading a path from untrusted input such as a config file or query parameter) can load an arbitrary .node native addon from any filesystem path, including network shares or temp directories. There is no extension check, no canonical-path check, and no allowlist. The only guard is ensureBindingLoaded(), which checks the loaded module's version string — a crafted malicious addon can easily pass that check.
evidence: Verified in index.ts:278: `require(candidate)` is called directly with the caller-supplied path. No extension, canonical, or allowlist check is performed. The `ensureBindingLoaded` check is trivially bypassable by a crafted addon that returns a non-'scaffold' version string and `loaded: true`. This is a real arbitrary native code execution path when `loadNativeBinding` options come from untrusted input.

category: decompression bomb / missing input size cap
title: Default maxPixels of 1<<28 (268 million pixels) allows decompression bombs for callers who omit the field
description: createNativeCodecFacade().createDecoder() merges a default of maxPixels: 1 << 28 (268,435,456 pixels ≈ 16384×16384 at 4 bytes = ~1 GB of output). Any caller that does not explicitly pass a lower maxPixels will silently accept images up to that limit.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:342 sets the default to `1 << 28` (268 million pixels, ~1 GB for rgba8). A JXL file claiming 16384×16384 dimensions passes the guard, and the native decoder allocates the full output buffer before any pixel limit fires. The default is high enough to be dangerous in adversarial or untrusted-input contexts.

title: Stub detection relies on fragile substring checks that can be bypassed by a crafted version string or probe path
description: ensureBindingLoaded() uses version().includes('scaffold') and probe.path.toLowerCase().includes('stub') to reject placeholder addons.
evidence: Verified in index.ts:461-471: the stub guard uses `includes('scaffold')` on the version string, which any real-but-malformed addon can avoid. Additionally, a legitimate addon in a path containing 'stub' in a parent directory name would be incorrectly rejected. The check is defensive-in-intent but not a genuine integrity guarantee.

category: no validation of native escape hatch inputs
title: advancedFrameSettings ids and values are forwarded to native C++ without any range or allowlist check
description: normalizeEncoderOptions() collects advancedFrameSettings entries (caller-supplied id/value pairs) and merges them with convenience-field-derived entries before forwarding to the native addon. No check is performed on id values or value ranges before they reach the C++ JxlEncoderFrameSettingSetFloatValue / JxlEncoderFrameSettingSetValue calls.
evidence: Verified: normalizeEncoderOptions at index.ts:301 blindly spreads caller-supplied advancedFrameSettings. guardEncoderOptions at line 373-374 is explicitly empty. Out-of-range frame setting IDs passed to libjxl's JxlEncoderFrameSettingSetValue are defined as returning JXL_ENC_ERROR, but the native.cc error handling for that path is not audited here. The escape hatch is fully unguarded at the JS layer.

category: secrets / developer paths in committed test code
title: Hardcoded absolute developer machine paths committed to version control expose internal directory layout
description: Lines 5-6 hardcode absolute Windows paths (C:\Foo\raw-converter\target\release\build\jpegxl-sys-26f294f2024eaecb\out\include and C:\TEMP\jxl-mt-libs) as fallback defaults when env vars are absent.
evidence: Verified in codec.test.ts:4-6: the hardcoded fallback paths including build artifact hash `jpegxl-sys-26f294f2024eaecb` and `C:\TEMP\jxl-mt-libs` are committed to version control. These expose the developer's filesystem layout in repo history, CI logs, and clones.

title: guardEncoderOptions is intentionally empty — there is no validation convention for EncoderOptions fields passed to native
description: guardDecoderOptions() validates region bounds but guardEncoderOptions() is an empty stub.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:373-374 confirms guardEncoderOptions is explicitly empty. width/height of 0, negative distance, effort outside 1-9, and other invalid values pass through to native without JS-side validation.

title: DecoderFinalize leaks pinned_input napi_ref when decoder is GC'd without dispose()
description: DecoderFinalize (called by the GC finalizer) iterates data->events and deletes them but does NOT call release_pinned_decoder. If a caller creates a decoder, pushes a chunk (pinning it via napi_create_reference), then lets the object be GC'd without calling dispose() or close(), the napi_ref in pinned_input is never deleted. This pins the JS ArrayBuffer indefinitely, preventing its memory from being freed by V8.
evidence: Verified at native.cc:1913-1918: DecoderFinalize deletes event refs but does not call `release_pinned_decoder(env, data)`. The `release_pinned_decoder` function exists and is called in DecoderClose (1646, 1696), DecoderDispose (1727), and the decode error path (1691) — but not in the GC finalizer. If a decoder is GC'd after push() but without close()/dispose(), the napi_ref at `data->pinned_input` permanently pins the input ArrayBuffer.

title: EncoderFinalize leaks multi-push encoder pixel/icc/exif/xmp/extra_channels/frames/custom_boxes vectors when GC'd without dispose()
description: EncoderFinalize (the GC finalizer) only deletes data->chunks refs and the pinned_input ref, then calls delete data. It does not shrink-to-empty the large pixel/metadata/frame vectors the way EncoderDispose does (via swap idiom).
evidence: `delete data` at line 1927 invokes `EncoderData`'s destructor, which correctly frees all `std::vector` members (pixels, icc, exif, xmp, extra_channels, frames, custom_boxes) via RAII. The swap idiom in EncoderDispose is an eager release to reduce peak memory during explicit dispose; the finalizer's `delete data` achieves equivalent deallocation. No memory leak exists — the heap is reclaimed on GC.

title: cachedBinding module-level singleton is written without guard after concurrent callers read it as null
description: loadNativeBinding() reads cachedBinding (line 268), then may perform expensive require() work across loop iterations, then writes cachedBinding (line 290). In Node.js this is safe for synchronous paths only. However, the function is exposed as part of a synchronous public API and tests show it is called from multiple createDecoder/createEncoder call sites that could be invoked concurrently (e.g. from worker_threads).
evidence: Node.js worker_threads each have their own module registry, so `require()` and module-level state is per-thread; `cachedBinding` is not shared across worker threads. Within a single thread, `loadNativeBinding` is synchronous and JS is single-threaded, so no true race exists. However if the package is used via a shared context or shared memory pattern, the concern could be real. Not enough cross-file context to rule it out entirely.

title: decoder.close() promise not awaited before expect() in decompression-bomb test — assertion may fire before rejection
description: The test at line 496 calls `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)` but does not `await` the expectation.
code_excerpt:
await decoder.push(encoded);
    expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow("image exceeds maxPixels");
evidence: Duplicate of logic-4; same code location confirmed. Missing `await` on the rejects assertion means the test passes vacuously, leaving the decompression-bomb guard untested.

title: DecoderCancel sets flag but does not release pinned_input reference, leaving it pinned until close/dispose
description: DecoderCancel (line 1711) sets data->cancelled = true but does not call release_pinned_decoder. If a consumer calls cancel() and then never calls close() or dispose(), the pinned napi_ref on the input ArrayBuffer leaks for the object's lifetime.
evidence: Verified at native.cc:1711-1716: DecoderCancel only sets `data->cancelled = true`. The `release_pinned_decoder` call needed to free the napi_ref is absent. DecoderClose handles this correctly at line 1646 on the cancel path, but if the caller never calls close() after cancel() (relying on GC), the napi_ref remains pinned — and as shown by concurrency-1, DecoderFinalize also does not call release_pinned_decoder.

title: EncoderData memory leaked when custom box type validation fails mid-loop after earlier boxes already reserved capacity
description: In CreateEncoder (line 2163-2180), when a custom box has a type string of incorrect length the function calls `delete data` and returns an error.
evidence: The finding itself states 'Current code is safe' — `delete data` at line 2170 invokes the EncoderData destructor which frees all vector members via RAII. The `release_pinned(env, data)` call before delete handles the napi_ref. No memory leak exists; the pattern is safe C++ RAII cleanup.

title: wrapDecoder's push method is synchronous but NativeDecoder interface declares it as void | Promise<void> — callers that await push() and check for errors will silently swallow throws
description: The wrapped decoder's push method (line 382) is `(chunk) => raw.push(chunk)` — a plain forwarding function.
evidence: The arrow function `(chunk) => raw.push(chunk)` returns the result of `raw.push(chunk)` — if native returns a rejected Promise, the wrapper returns that Promise to the caller. The finding's concern about callers using `.catch()` is valid only if native push() actually returns a Promise (the current implementation is synchronous and returns void). No concrete failure scenario exists with the current native implementation.

title: NativeDecoder/NativeEncoder have no AbortSignal integration — cancel() must be called manually with no composable cancellation
description: Both NativeDecoder and NativeEncoder expose a cancel(reason?) method but there is no way to wire a standard AbortSignal to trigger cancel automatically.
evidence: Verified: neither NativeDecoder nor NativeEncoder interfaces nor the wrapDecoder shim accept an AbortSignal. Callers must manually bridge AbortController.signal.addEventListener('abort', ...) to cancel(), creating boilerplate and risking leaks when the surrounding fetch/request is aborted.

title: loadNativeBinding swallows all candidate errors and attaches only the last one as `cause`
description: When both candidate paths fail the loop discards all but the last error and throws `CapabilityMissing` with only `lastError` as `cause`. If the prebuilt path fails with a meaningful diagnostic (e.g. wrong ABI, glibc mismatch) but the source path fails with a generic ENOENT, the useful error is silently dropped.
evidence: Verified in index.ts:275-297: the `lastError` variable is overwritten on each iteration; only the last failure is surfaced as `cause`. A meaningful ABI/glibc error from the prebuilt path is swallowed when the source path produces a plain ENOENT, making CI failures hard to diagnose.

title: resolvePrebuiltBinary accesses `process.platform` and `process.arch` with optional chaining but then passes a potentially-`undefined` string into `resolve()`
description: When `process` is unavailable (e.g. non-Node host), `process?.platform` evaluates to `undefined` and `?? "unknown"` kicks in, making the candidate path `<base>/unknown-unknown/jxl-native.node`.
evidence: The code correctly uses `process?.platform ?? "unknown"` — if `process` is absent, optional chaining returns `undefined` and the nullish coalescing operator substitutes `"unknown"`. The fallback path `unknown-unknown/jxl-native.node` will fail with ENOENT in require(), which is caught by the loop in loadNativeBinding. The `?? "unknown"` fallback works exactly as intended. No crash or silent misconfiguration occurs.

title: wrapDecoder.push() is not awaited and its result is not returned, so a rejected promise from the native push() is silently dropped
description: The `push` wrapper at line 382 calls `raw.push(chunk)` and returns `void` — it neither `await`s nor returns the result.
evidence: The arrow function `(chunk) => raw.push(chunk)` is an implicit return — it returns the result of `raw.push(chunk)`. If `raw.push` returns a Promise, that Promise is returned to the caller. The claim that 'the result is not returned' is incorrect; the arrow function does return it.

title: release() called in finally for close/cancel/dispose but not for push(), leaving inputDone unresolved if push throws synchronously before close is called
description: If `raw.push()` throws synchronously and the caller never calls `close()`, `cancel()`, or `dispose()`, the `inputDone` promise never resolves. Any consumer that calls `events()` will then `await inputDone` forever, producing a permanent hang.
evidence: Verified: `events()` at line 392-395 awaits `inputDone` before yielding. If `raw.push()` throws synchronously and the caller omits close/cancel/dispose (e.g. catches the push error and abandons the decoder), `release()` is never called, `inputDone` never resolves, and `events()` hangs indefinitely. This is a real hang scenario in error-recovery code paths.

title: wrapDecoder double-wrap guard uses a duck-type property `__jxlWrappedEvents` that can be trivially defeated and does not protect against wrapping twice via different code paths
description: The guard `(raw as any).__jxlWrappedEvents` is checked before wrapping. The finding notes the current behavior is correct but the fragility of the sentinel means any future refactor that resets it will silently double-wrap.
code_excerpt:
function wrapDecoder(raw: NativeDecoder): NativeDecoder {
  if ((raw as any).__jxlWrappedEvents) return raw;
evidence: Verified: index.ts:377-378 uses `__jxlWrappedEvents` as a duck-type sentinel. The finding's severity is appropriately low — double-wrapping would cause events() to await inputDone twice and then yield from an already-drained iterator, silently producing no events. The sentinel is correct today but is not type-safe and is fragile to refactoring.

title: normalizeEncoderOptions silently ignores duplicate `advancedFrameSettings` entries for the same setting id when merged from convenience fields
description: The check `!adv.some(x => x.id === id)` only skips adding the convenience field if the id already exists in the caller-supplied `advancedFrameSettings`. However it does not validate that the caller-supplied array itself contains duplicate ids.
evidence: Verified: normalizeEncoderOptions at line 301 spreads caller's advancedFrameSettings verbatim. The dedup check at line 311 only prevents convenience fields from duplicating existing entries, not caller-supplied duplicates from being passed through. Libjxl processes frame settings in order with last-write-wins semantics, so duplicates produce silent, unpredictable behavior.

title: guardDecoderOptions does not validate that the region fits within image dimensions, so an oversized region is silently passed to libjxl
description: The guard checks that `w > 0`, `h > 0`, `x >= 0`, `y >= 0` but does not validate that `x + w` or `y + h` fit within the image.
evidence: Verified: guardDecoderOptions only checks sign/zero of w/h/x/y. An oversized region passes the guard and reaches native code where libjxl's transform_fused_into clamps via EffectiveRegion arithmetic. The native behavior is defined (clamping), but the JS guard gives a false sense of safety. Adding a documentation comment about native clamping behavior would address the confusion.

category: test: missing error event check / silent decode failure
title: maxPixels bomb-guard test uses `.rejects.toThrow()` on `decoder.close()` without awaiting it, so the assertion may pass vacuously
description: Line 496 calls `expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow(...)` without `await`. If `decoder.close()` is synchronous (returns `void` not a `Promise`), `.rejects` receives a non-Promise and Bun/Jest will resolve it as a fulfilled value rather than a rejection.
code_excerpt:
await decoder.push(encoded);
    expect(decoder.close()).rejects.toThrow("image exceeds maxPixels");
evidence: Duplicate of logic-4 and concurrency-4; same line 496 confirmed. The missing `await` is a real defect — the decompression-bomb guard test is silently non-functional regardless of whether close() is sync or async.

category: test: undeclared fields used without type guard
title: facade.test.ts passes `codestreamLevel`, `advancedControls`, and `ecResampling` fields that are not declared on `EncoderOptions` in index.ts, with no runtime or type-level guard in the production path
description: The tests exercise codestreamLevel, advancedControls, ecResampling via type assertions, but these fields are not present in src/index.ts EncoderOptions. The tests work around this with casts.
code_excerpt:
facade.createEncoder({ ...encodeOptions, codestreamLevel: 10 } as typeof encodeOptions & { codestreamLevel: 10 });
    expect((received as { codestreamLevel?: number }).codestreamLevel).toBe(10);
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts EncoderOptions (lines 154-202) lacks codestreamLevel, advancedControls, ecResampling; dist/index.d.ts lines 294-359 declare all three. The tests use `as` casts to work around the src type gap. This confirms contracts-9 and shows that src is the stale interface — fields exercised in tests are only in dist, not the source of truth.

title: Only one error class (CapabilityMissing) exists; native decode errors, encode errors, and image-too-large all surface as generic JS errors with no structured code field
description: The `{ type: 'error'; code: string; message: string }` decode event carries a `code` string from the native layer, but there is no TypeScript error class hierarchy for these codes.
evidence: Verified: only `CapabilityMissing` exists in index.ts. Decode errors (ImageTooLarge, DecodeFailed, etc.) arrive as `{ type: 'error'; code: string }` events — callers must string-match on code. No typed error classes make catch blocks brittle across libjxl version changes.

title: src/index.ts DecodeEvent and DecodedExtraChannel types diverge from dist/index.d.ts shipped contract
description: The shipped type declaration at dist/index.d.ts defines DecodedExtraChannel with a mandatory 'pixels: ArrayBuffer' and 'pixelFormat: PixelFormat' field. src/index.ts defines DecodedExtraChannel as 'Readonly<Omit<ExtraChannel, ...>>' which has no pixels/pixelFormat. Additionally, dist declares 'extraChannelDescriptors?' on progress/final events, whereas src uses 'extraChannels?'.
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts:72 defines `DecodedExtraChannel = Readonly<Omit<ExtraChannel, ...>>` (no pixels/pixelFormat); dist/index.d.ts:32-35 defines it extending ExtraChannelDescriptor with `pixels: ArrayBuffer` and `pixelFormat: PixelFormat`. Progress/final events in src use `extraChannels?` (line 95, 109); dist uses `extraChannelDescriptors?` (line 63, 81). These are incompatible shapes — consumers compiled against dist will find fields missing at runtime.

title: src DecoderOptions is missing fields that dist/index.d.ts DecoderOptions omits — but both omit decodeExtraChannels/maxPixels/progressiveDetail that the runtime requires
description: The public DecoderOptions in src/index.ts declares decodeExtraChannels, maxPixels, and progressiveDetail as optional fields. The dist/index.d.ts DecoderOptions has none of these fields.
evidence: Verified: dist/index.d.ts:97-105 DecoderOptions ends at `preserveMetadata: boolean` with no progressiveDetail, decodeExtraChannels, or maxPixels. src/index.ts:119-133 includes all three. Tests in codec.test.ts pass `maxPixels: 1` and `decodeExtraChannels: true` at runtime; consumers importing from the package dist would get type errors on these fields despite runtime acceptance.

title: AnimationOptions.ticksPerSecond is required in src but optional in dist/index.d.ts
description: src/index.ts declares AnimationOptions as '{ ticksPerSecond: number; loopCount?: number; }' (ticksPerSecond required). dist/index.d.ts declares 'ticksPerSecond?: number' (optional).
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts:145 declares `ticksPerSecond: number` (required); dist/index.d.ts:245 declares `ticksPerSecond?: number` (optional). Callers using dist types can omit ticksPerSecond without a compile error, potentially producing a libjxl default ticks-per-second that differs from intent.

title: budget_exceeded DecodeEvent carries extraChannels in src but not in dist, creating consumer split
description: In src/index.ts the budget_exceeded event variant includes 'extraChannels?: readonly DecodedExtraChannel[]'. In dist/index.d.ts the budget_exceeded variant has no extraChannels field.
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts:116 includes `extraChannels?` on budget_exceeded; dist/index.d.ts:85-96 budget_exceeded has no extraChannels. Consumers compiled against dist who handle budget_exceeded and inspect extra-channel state will not get a type-safe field, and the runtime value will be silently `undefined` in TypeScript's view.

title: ExtraChannelType in src includes 'thermal', 'reserved0..7', and 'unknown' but dist ExtraChannel.type only allows 'alpha'|'depth'|'spot'|'selection'|'other'
description: src/index.ts defines ExtraChannelType as a wide union including 'thermal', 'reserved0'–'reserved7', and 'unknown'. dist/index.d.ts restricts ExtraChannel.type to 'alpha'|'depth'|'spot'|'selection'|'other'.
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts:38-45 defines wide ExtraChannelType including thermal/reserved*/unknown; dist/index.d.ts:180 has `type: 'alpha' | 'depth' | 'spot' | 'selection' | 'other'`. The src comment says this is intentional for Task 5 forward compat parity, but the dist doesn't expose these types — a breaking schema difference for any real JXL file that has thermal or reserved channels.

title: guardEncoderOptions is a no-op — EncoderOptions has no input validation despite several invariants
description: guardEncoderOptions on line 373 is empty ('No capability guards remain'). EncoderOptions has several fields where invalid inputs can produce hard-to-debug native crashes or silent wrong output.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:373-374 — guardEncoderOptions is explicitly empty. Width/height of 0, simultaneous non-null distance and quality (undefined precedence), and effort values outside the 1-9 TypeScript union all pass through to native code without any JS-side validation producing opaque native errors.

title: NativeDecoder interface in src omits seekToFrame/seekToTime but wrapDecoder attaches them unconditionally
description: The NativeDecoder interface in src/index.ts (lines 240-246) declares only push/close/events/cancel/dispose. wrapDecoder (lines 377-411) unconditionally attaches seekToFrame and seekToTime to every wrapped decoder. dist/index.d.ts NativeDecoder does declare these as optional methods.
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts:240-246 NativeDecoder has no seekToFrame/seekToTime; wrapDecoder at lines 398-409 attaches both. dist/index.d.ts:388-393 correctly declares them as optional. Code checking `typeof decoder.seekToFrame === 'function'` against the src type would get a TypeScript error despite the runtime object having the method.

title: normalizeEncoderOptions silently merges advancedFrameSettings with no duplicate-ID guard
description: normalizeEncoderOptions builds the 'adv' array by appending entries from opts.advancedFrameSettings and then adding named-shortcut entries only if no existing entry has the same id. However, if the caller passes advancedFrameSettings with duplicate IDs, those duplicates pass through unchecked.
evidence: Duplicate of errors-6; same code path confirmed. Caller-supplied duplicate IDs in advancedFrameSettings are spread verbatim into the native call. Libjxl applies them in order (last-wins), producing silent and unexpected frame setting behavior for callers who accidentally duplicate IDs.

title: EncoderOptions in src lacks codestreamLevel, advancedControls, ecResampling, premultiply and many other fields present in dist — tests that exercise those fields cast around the missing types
description: facade.test.ts lines 62-106 exercise codestreamLevel, advancedControls (with buffering.streamingInput), and ecResampling. None of these fields are declared in src/index.ts EncoderOptions.
evidence: Verified: src/index.ts EncoderOptions (154-202) lacks codestreamLevel, advancedControls, ecResampling, premultiply, decodingSpeed, etc. dist/index.d.ts:248-376 EncoderOptions has all of these. The test type casts confirm src is significantly behind dist. The src is the compile-time source but is not authoritative for callers.

title: No structural contract test verifies src/index.ts types match dist/index.d.ts at build time
description: The divergence between src/index.ts and dist/index.d.ts (findings contracts-1 through contracts-9) went undetected because there is no build-time check that the compiled dist matches the src.
code_excerpt:
// No test file in packages/jxl-native/test/ imports from '../dist/index.d.ts'
// or cross-checks src and dist types for assignability.
evidence: Verified: the src vs dist divergence confirmed across contracts-1 through contracts-9 is extensive and real. A tsd/dtslint check or single-source CI generation would have caught all of these. The absence of such a check is the root cause.

title: normalizeEncoderOptions spreads the full options object and rebuilds two arrays on every encode call
description: Every call to createEncoder calls normalizeEncoderOptions, which does `[...(opts.advancedFrameSettings ?? [])]` to copy the entire advancedFrameSettings array, then `extraChannels.map(...)` to copy the extraChannels array, then `{ ...opts }` to shallow-clone the entire EncoderOptions object.
evidence: Verified: normalizeEncoderOptions performs three allocations per call (adv spread, extraChannels.map, options spread). Combined with the double-normalization confirmed in logic-2, this happens twice per createEncoder invocation via the top-level export. For batch/pyramid pipelines encoding many images, this adds measurable GC pressure.

title: Linear scan over advancedFrameSettings with Array.some() on every convenience-field lowering
description: normalizeEncoderOptions iterates over up to 6 convenience fields. For each one that is set it calls `adv.some(x => x.id === id)` which is a full linear scan over the existing advancedFrameSettings array.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:309-314 performs a linear scan per convenience field. With N=6 convenience fields and M typical advancedFrameSettings entries (usually 0-5), this is O(N*M)=O(30) per encode. Trivial for single calls but fires on every batch encode invocation, compounded by double-normalization.

title: loadNativeBinding called redundantly on every createDecoder / createEncoder invocation but only cached for the non-custom case
description: The convenience functions createDecoder and createEncoder (lines 355-361) each call createNativeCodecFacade(loadNativeBinding()) on every call. createNativeCodecFacade itself always constructs a fresh closure object.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:355-361 creates a fresh facade closure object on every createDecoder/createEncoder call. The binding itself is cached via cachedBinding, but the facade object `{ createDecoder, createEncoder }` is allocated fresh each time. For high-frequency batch callers this is avoidable allocation.

title: wrapDecoder allocates a new Promise and three async closures on every decoder construction
description: wrapDecoder creates a fresh `new Promise<void>` plus the `release` closure plus at least three async wrapper functions (close, cancel, dispose) and two async generator shims (seekToFrame, seekToTime) for every NativeDecoder instance.
evidence: Verified: wrapDecoder at lines 377-411 allocates one Promise, one release closure, and five method closures (close, cancel, dispose, events generator, seekToFrame/seekToTime shims) per decoder. For batch thumbnail generation with many short-lived decoders, these 7-8 allocations per decode contribute to GC pressure.

title: Multiple test cases each call readFileSync on index.ts to check for string presence — repeated synchronous disk reads
description: codec.test.ts has at least 7 test cases that each call `readFileSync(new URL('../src/index.ts', import.meta.url), 'utf8')` independently. Each call does a full synchronous filesystem read of the entire index.ts source file for a simple string-contains check.
evidence: Verified in codec.test.ts: multiple test cases each independently import `readFileSync` and read index.ts. The file content is identical on each read. Moving the read to module scope would eliminate repeated synchronous I/O in the test suite.

title: facade.test.ts reads native.cc source synchronously inside each test instead of at module load
description: Two test cases each call `readFileSync(new URL('../src/native.cc', import.meta.url), 'utf8')` independently. native.cc is a large C++ file.
evidence: Verified in facade.test.ts: two tests each read the full native.cc file independently. At several thousand lines, native.cc is substantially larger than index.ts, making the redundancy more costly.

title: fileExists uses synchronous accessSync in the module's binary resolution path
description: resolveSourceBinary calls fileExists twice (for Release and Debug paths), each using accessSync which is synchronous filesystem I/O. This runs on module load inside loadNativeBinding.
evidence: Verified: index.ts:451-458 uses `accessSync` which blocks the event loop. Called during the first `loadNativeBinding()` invocation (before cachedBinding is set), this blocks the event loop for two filesystem round-trips. A one-time cost, but synchronous I/O in an otherwise async-capable library is noteworthy.

title: No encode/decode throughput benchmarks exist for the native codec path
description: All tests in codec.test.ts exercise correctness only (round-trip, extra channels, animation). There is no timing measurement, no throughput baseline, and no regression gate for the native libjxl encode/decode performance.
evidence: Verified: codec.test.ts contains no timing or throughput assertions. The MEMORY.md notes a documented case where a build flag change (/O2 missing from cmake) caused a ~30× slowdown that went undetected until manual observation — exactly the failure mode a benchmark gate would catch.
